verbatim_quote: Not … not loved. Mayhaps wanted is a better word, but … it was only kitchen gossip, the whispers of washerwomen and stableboys …
note: Barristan to Daenerys about Aerys. 'wanted' is exact word used.

verbatim_quote: Prince Aerys … as a youth, he was taken with a certain lady of Casterly Rock, a cousin of Tywin Lannister. When she and Tywin wed, your father drank too much wine at the wedding feast and was heard to say that it was a great pity that the lord's right to the first night had been abolished. A drunken jape, no more, but Tywin Lannister was not a man to forget such words, or the … the liberties your father took during the bedding.
note: Exact match: 'liberties' at the wedding-night incident as paraphrased.
Beat asserts a negative claim ('wasn't sex', 'too early for conception'). No book passage can assert what did NOT happen. Plausible interpretation of the Barristan passage, but not a grounded quote.

verbatim_quote: Not … not loved. Mayhaps wanted is a better word, but … it was only kitchen gossip, the whispers of washerwomen and stableboys …
note: Same Barristan passage establishes Aerys 'wanted' Joanna and 'took liberties' at wedding, supporting the beat's claim that Aerys lusted after her.

Searched ASOS Sansa chapters (B05 claims 'Joffrey tells Sansa that kings often sleep with whoever they want, including married women'). Sansa IV contains wedding-feast banter but no explicit statement matching this paraphrase. May be paraphrased/misremembered from show rather than book, or may be a TWOW reference not yet published.

Claim: 'questioned whether Joanna was even at King's Landing when Aerys lusted after her.' No book passage questions Joanna's location at conception-time. The Barristan passage implies she was at the wedding (timeline consistent with Tywin/Joanna marrying at KL and Tyrion born later), but doesn't address conceptional timing debate.

Claim: 'Tywin was Hand, Joanna must have been with Tywin if that's when Tyrion was conceived.' Books do not place Joanna with Tywin during his Handship. No grounded passage establishes concurrent location-timing.

Claim: 'Joanna was apparently at Casterly Rock shortly before giving birth to Tyrion.' No book passage uses 'shortly' in this context or establishes Joanna's late-pregnancy location precisely enough to ground this beat.

Speculative claim: 'It's conceivable Tywin never knew Tyrion wasn't his biological son.' Books do not explore this possibility; only Tywin's actions (harsh treatment) suggest possible knowledge or suspicion.

verbatim_quote: You . . . you are no . . . no son of mine.
note: Tywin's explicit disowning of Tyrion. Video claims this is Tywin 'outright says it a couple of times' — this is one instance.

Meta-claim: video argues Tywin's hatred must have a deeper cause than Tyrion killing Joanna. No book passage supports this *argument*; books only show Tywin's cruelty and disowning, not the video's interpretive frame.

verbatim_quote: Lord Tywin had put an end to that hope ten days before his dwarf son's sixteenth nameday, when Tyrion asked to tour the Nine Free Cities, as his uncles had done at that same age. "My brothers could be relied upon to bring no shame upon House Lannister," his father had replied. "Neither ever wed a whore." And when Tyrion had reminded him that in ten days he would be a man grown, free to travel where he wished, Lord Tywin had said, "No man is free. Only children and fools think elsewise. Go, by all means. Wear motley and stand upon your head to amuse the spice lords and the cheese kings. Just see that you pay your own way and put aside any thoughts of returning." At that the boy's defiance had crumbled. "If it is useful occupation you require, useful occupation you shall have," his father then said. So to mark his manhood, Tyrion was given charge of all the drains and cisterns within Casterly Rock. Perhaps he hoped I'd fall into one. But Tywin had been disappointed in that. The drains never drained half so well as when he had charge of them.
note: Exact match to input's already-matched passage.

Comparative claim: 'Jaime and Cersei have personality defects but Tywin doesn't mistreat them without good reason.' Books do not provide a comparative framework showing Tywin's *reasons* for favoring/disfavoring each child.

Claim: 'Tywin disowns Tyrion despite legacy obsession, unless Tyrion isn't his true heir.' Interpretive argument, not a grounded quote. Books show disowning + legacy obsession, but don't explicitly frame disowning as proof of non-paternity.

Complex causal claim: 'Aerys making Jaime a Kingsguard would have left Aerys's bastard Tyrion as heir to Tywin's legacy, explaining Tywin's harsh treatment and sacking of KL.' No book passage links these causal chains explicitly. Requires inference.
Speculative claim: 'Tywin lets Tyrion live because he's still Joanna's son, like a deathbed promise.' Books do not mention a deathbed promise or this as Tywin's reason for tolerating Tyrion.

Claim: 'Tywin funds Tyrion generously and periodically tries to get him killed (e.g., Blackwater placement).' Books show both funding and harsh treatment, but no explicit authorial link to assassination attempts.

Claim: 'Targaryen dragon dreams are common — Daenerys, Aemon, Daeron, Daemon all had prophetic dragon dreams.' Daenerys does dream of dragons; Aemon and Daemon D are known to have dreamed of dragons. But books do not explicitly frame all these as 'prophetic.' Requires interpretation of dream-significance.

Claim: 'Tyrion is linked to dragons, but has no literal prophetic dragon dreams like Targaryens — likely he just likes dragons.' Books show Tyrion's love of dragons and dragon-lore but do not compare his non-dreaming to Targaryen dreaming explicitly.

verbatim_quote: The seed is strong, Jon Arryn had cried on his deathbed, and so it was. All those bastards, all with hair as black as night.
note: Exact principle match. The 'seed is strong' principle is established by Jon Arryn as a theory of heredity (strong traits override weak ones). Tyrion's pale-blond hair is mentioned separately (ADWD). This passage establishes the *principle*, not Tyrion's specific hair color.

Claim: 'Tyrion has one green eye and one black eye; Shiera Seastar is the only other character with mismatched eyes, linking two-colored eyes to mixed Targaryen blood.' Books mention Tyrion's mismatched eyes (AGOT, AFFC) and Shiera Seastar exists in canon, but no passage links mismatched eyes to Targaryen blood or draws the symbolic connection the video asserts.
As marked in input: video speculation about Targaryen genetic weirdness or abortion. Not a book-grounded claim.
Claim: 'Tyrion could fit the three heads of the dragon alongside Dany and Jon.' This is fan-theory inference about prophecy, not grounded in book text as an explicit claim.
Meta-claim: 'Video questions whether prophecy functions reliably in the story.' This is the video's argumentative frame, not a book quote to be grounded.

verbatim_quote: "Jaime," she said, tugging on his ear, "sweetling, I have known you since you were a babe at Joanna's breast. You smile like Gerion and fight like Tyg, and there's some of Kevan in you, else you would not wear that cloak . . . but Tyrion is Tywin's son, not you. I said so once to your father's face, and he would not speak to me for half a year. Men are such thundering great fools. Even the sort who come along once in a thousand years."
note: Genna Lannister's statement that Tyrion is Tywin's true son (in temperament/cunning), NOT literal parentage. Contradicts A+J=T theory if read literally, but video frames it as Genna recognizing Tyrion's similarity to Tywin's character, not as evidence against Targaryen parentage.

Claim: 'Tyrion's drinking and whoring are consequences of Tywin's abuse; Tywin hates him for vices Tywin shares.' Interpretive analysis, not a grounded quote. Books show both phenomena but not explicit causal link or Tywin's self-awareness of his own vices.

verbatim_quote: My lord father had no use for whores, she thought. After our mother died he never touched a woman. She gave the guardsman a chilly look. "This is not . . . when Lord Tywin's father died he returned to Casterly Rock to find a . . . a woman of this sort . . . bedecked in his lady mother's jewels, wearing one of her gowns. He stripped them off her, and all else as well. For a fortnight she was paraded naked through the streets of Lannisport, to confess to every man she met that she was a thief and a harlot. That was how Lord Tywin Lannister dealt with whores. He never . . . this woman was here for some other purpose, not for . . ."
note: Exact match to input's already-matched passage. Cersei's memory of Tywin's father's mistress and Tywin's brutal response.

verbatim_quote: You . . . you are no . . . no son of mine.
note: Tywin's disowning statement. Video interprets this as rejection of Tyrion's twisted body/mirror-of-self, NOT as literal proof of illegitimacy. Grounded quote supports both interpretations; the video's frame is argumentative.
